phrases.intent_en: Ask about the famous literary cafés
phrases.text_local: Où est le Café de Flore ? Et Les Deux Magots ?
phrases.pronunciation: oo eh luh kah-FAY duh FLOHR? ay lay duh mah-GOH
phrases.cultural_note: Both cafés face the church of Saint-Germain-des-Prés on Boulevard Saint-Germain. Sartre and Beauvoir held court at Flore; Hemingway preferred Les Deux Magots. Expect to pay 7+ euros for a coffee.

phrases.intent_en: Ask about the neighborhood's intellectual history
phrases.text_local: C'est ici que Sartre et Beauvoir venaient ?
phrases.pronunciation: seh ee-SEE kuh SARTR ay boh-VWAHR vuh-NEH
phrases.cultural_note: This was the epicenter of existentialism in the 1940s-50s. Sartre, Beauvoir, Camus, and later the jazz scene made this the intellectual heart of Paris.

phrases.text_local: Connaissez-vous une bonne librairie dans le quartier ?
phrases.pronunciation: koh-neh-SAY voo oon bohn lee-breh-REE dahn luh kar-TYAY
phrases.cultural_note: La Hune (now closed) was legendary, but try Librairie Gallimard or the many antique book dealers on Rue de Seine. The bouquinistes along the Seine are just steps away.

phrases.intent_en: Ask about the church of Saint-Germain-des-Prés
phrases.text_local: L'église de Saint-Germain, c'est la plus vieille de Paris ?
phrases.pronunciation: lay-GLEEZ duh sahn-zhehr-MAHN, seh lah ploo vyeh-yuh duh pah-REE
phrases.cultural_note: Founded in 558 AD, it's the oldest church in Paris. The current Romanesque structure dates to the 11th century. Free entry — look for the recently restored colorful murals inside.

phrases.intent_en: Ask about antique galleries on Rue de Seine
phrases.text_local: Il y a beaucoup de galeries d'art dans cette rue ?
phrases.pronunciation: eel ee ah boh-KOO duh gah-leh-REE DAR dahn set ROO
phrases.cultural_note: Rue de Seine and surrounding streets have dozens of art galleries — from old masters to contemporary. Window shopping is free and galleries welcome browsers.
